transcript.transcript: This is Sadie Brooeon interviewing Elsie Lenz Johnson. September 18, 1989. And the subject is logging pioneering from 1920 until 1975. Elsie, as the wife of a logging pioneer, tell me about those early days. Well, the first time I met Mister Johnson, we were both 16, and they lived, we lived in cougar guff. Both of us lived on farms. He was a plum branch of the Cougar Gulch and we were on the other branch. And that was our way of living. That was our way of making a living out there was either making cord wood or hauling logs, seemed to give us spending money. And Johnson's, they had a team of, two teams of horses. So in the winter months, him and the boys would cut and haul logs in the summer months. They made cordwood, and in the winter months, they haul logs. And they'd haul them with the sled and the horses. Would do it in the winter months because you would load more logs on and get more of them on. It was easier than doing it by wagon. And of course, coming down the hill at Levendale was so easy because there's no brakes on sleds. And when you're loaded, you'd be pushing on the horses while they'd start running and very soon galloping, because there'd be more than they could hold back on. But they always seemed to make it without any accidents of any kind. And then, of course, when they got down in the flat part of the country, they had to pull them. And they took them into Coeur d'Alene. At that time there was a black whale mill. And that's where they sold their logs and made our spare money. Well, then as the boys got older, they went to, oh wait a minute, when they were coming down the hill one time, Mr. Johnson said, the horses were going at a gallop. They had a big load behind them and the horses just really got to going real good. They couldn't hold it back no more. Come around the turn, that's the mailman, He had a cutter and one horse. Told him after that he just drove right off into the off the road right down into the beach to get out of the way. And he said, boy he sure didn't like to be on that road when they was logging, because it just scared the death of the dead. But anyway, n one got hurt. And Ben Johnson, as he got older, went to work for Wittens out of Garwood. At that time, Wittens had a mill out at the head of the Coeur d'Alene River, and there was a train from Garwood, which would go into the camp, and they would haul the louse from the camp out on the train, and then we loaded them there, and they went from there to the mills. Winton's had a large barn, their guard with them, they kept their horses. I don't know how many teams of horses they had, but Anyway, they rented their horses and kept their horses right here in the winter months. And in the summer months, of course, they used the horses to skid the logs in the woods. The men would cut the logs with cut-cut saws, and when they had men that skidded, the company would train the horses, and the men would skid the logs in, and they'd put them on this train and bring them down to Calvert. Well then Mr. Johnson, my father, and others Went out there and they got to the first one that had it was a minor, but he decided to do some love And so he went to work for Witton's and they took it on contract We take the whole job and She took it to love step right down to the landing and then the company would load them on the train and haul them from there on out. But they would rent the horses from the company. They didn't go the horses. They rented the horses. Well, Mr. Johnson and that fellow worked with my father in the cell, and they could do a little bit better, that they'd get a money that way, so they all decided to kind of go into business for themselves. Well then the next, I think for two years then, they worked that way for Wittons out there at Garwood and then they, Wittons give them a large job up on Marble Creek on the St. Joe River. They would lay up on Marble Creek and there they flumed the logs out. And it was dry flumes. They made the flumes out of logs and they greased the flumes and would run the logs down this flume to the St. Joe River. The waters go right into the river, and then from there, of course, they'd flow down to St. Mary's. So, I think if I remember right, we worked out there, They worked out there one or two years. And the last year they were out there, Mr. Johnson, some way or another, missed the call. Because they would call up there when they put the logs in the flume, so if anyone was walking up the flume or was down the flume, the flume would be clear. But Mr. Johnson evidently didn't hear this call, and he was walking in the flume, and the first logs that went down hit him, broke both legs and he slipped. Well, there was no way of getting him out. There was no horses or cars or anything. They packed that man for two and a half miles down to the river, and then they had to put him on a tramway. They went across the river, tied him on the tramway, and pulled him across the river to catch the train out of St. Joe's to take him to the hospital to St. Mary's. And that man lay there, although he must have laid six or seven months in the hospital in Traction before they were able to take him home. But he got well and was able to walk. He got fine. He got over just fine. But it was a terrible... But that's the only way they had to get him down. And all the men worked together and packed him and got him on through. Well then after that job, then Mr. Johnson, there was a man by the name of Mr. Larson in St. Mary's that was an old-time logger, but he was a crack-attack. And he asked Mr. Johnson and Mr. Johnson wanted to go in with him to contract for logs. So then, I believe it was Wittens again, give them a contract up at the mouth of the Coeur d'Alene River, which was called Rock City then. And they went on in there and put, I don't recall the amount of logs, that the sawyers, they hired sawyers and then there was of course there was two men to a saw and they were six foot, six foot, six foot cross cut saws and there'd be two men on the saws, they would chip their trees, make a kind of a wedge on the tree, Whichever way they wanted that tree to fall, they would cut a wedge into that tree, then go behind that wedge on the other side of the tree, and cut through. Well, sometimes you'd get about halfway through that tree, and the tree would pinch the saw, and the saw wouldn't go nowhere. So then they always packed what they called little wedges, and they were wooden wedges, hardwood wedges, and they would pound the wedges in on the side where they started to saw. They would ease the pitch of the tree, and then they could finish cutting the tree. And just before they would get about an inch, maybe two inches, to where this chip was in the tree, then they would begin to get the wedges to make the tree start to crack and fall. And that's the way they fell the trees. Then they had the men with axes, usually the sawyers, the men that cut the trees, would cut all the limbs off of them and let them lay there. Or then they would cut them in length, so the long cut them. In, I think, then years it was 12 foot length. In the latter years, there was double that length. But the first years, it was just 12 foot length logs, because that's all they could handle. And then they would go with peens and skid them on into the landings. And the landings were usually wherever there was a chute, a dry chute that was made out of logs that they greased or else a chute where they turned water in and would run the logs to the creek or the river or wherever because they had no way of hauling them them days. Well then, that's the way they'd done it up in Marble Creek. And then when we went up to St. Joe, we went up to Slaughter House on St. Joe, And there we had, then they had their own tent, they made their own tents, which was tent tents. They put them on the floor, a wooden floor, and wooden sides up about three feet, and then the tent went over it. When the cookhouse was a tent, all your bunkhouses was a tent, and then of course your big wash house, they would go up the creek and make it, they always built them where there was a creek. They would make a little dam so they had backwater, and then they put their pipes in. They all had water, running water, in the cookhouse, also in the camp, and in the washroom because They had big boilers, they had stoves, and they had the hot water where the men could take their showers. They washed their own clothes. They bathed and done everything in the wash house. And of course they had their bunk houses. Usually in the bunk houses it would be from four to eight men in the double bunks. Each bunkhouse had a wooden stove and they had what they called a flunky that would cut wood just for the bunkhouses in the camp. He would sweep out the bunk houses, keep them clean. They were kept very clean. He would even scrub them once a week. He'd go in there with soap water in a bucket and scrub them out, clean them out. The same with the cook houses. And in the cook houses, of course, twice a week, the groceries come in. Usually Monday, there was a grocery truck come in, and Friday, they would go after grocery. And Everything come by the barrels and under pounds. While all along the cookhouse, where this three-foot wooden wall was, was lined with all the groceries. That's where all the groceries went. The canned stuff come in, all in gallon cans but in cases. They bought everything, fruit and vegetables, peas, corn, spring beans, all of your fruits and berries. Everything come in the gallon cans and those were all piled along this wall in the cook house. Well in front of this then was long benches and your long tables where the men sit and eat. And then of course the cook was on the end, where they washed the dishes. And then there's sinks they made them themselves. They were made out of boards, cot, had double sinks. One you washed in, the other one you drained your dishes in. Of course they had bloody hot water. And then there was a big drain where you put your cups in racks and your plates stood up in racks. And boiling water was thrown on them, they were sterilized, every meal, they were sterilized. The same with your silverware. Your silverware was put in a monstrous big... ...Closet with all holes at the bottom. That's where they washed their silverware. And then they scalded it the same way. And then we had, they had a blanket they put up on the table, a clean flannel blanket, and you dumped your silverware in there and left them right there. You didn't dry them because you had that hot water on them. They dry real quick. The air would dry them. That's the way you took care of your silverware. Everything was always sterilized with boiling water. Well then, when it comes to the cooks, you always had from two depending on how large your camps was. You had all the way from two to three cooks. You usually had your first cook and your second cook. The first cook, of course, would plan everything and help, but the second cook did most of the work. And then you had the baker. The baker would get up along about, oh, 2, 3 o'clock in the morning and start his bread because he baked every day he baked his bread. And they had dish pans, big steel pans that they put that dough in that I often wondered how the men could handle it. Well, up over the stove there was racks, and that's where they put their bread, up over these racks, for the heat so the bread would raise. Well, Then the cook then, after he got his bed started and he got the raisin, then he could go back to bed, the bakery cook. Because he had to come back about, oh, one o'clock then to make up his loaves and to start baking. Then, of course, he would make his bread, and he'd make his cinnamon rolls, and he'd make his Danish rolls. And when he got all of those made and in the oven and everything cooking, then he'd get the grease on and make you donuts. Donuts was cooked, was made fresh twice, sometimes three times a week. And then the cookies, they would always make their own olives from three to four kinds of cookies. They had the fruit bars, the molasses fruit bars that was out of this world. They always had the big sugar cookies, then the regular molasses cookies, And then there was all the oatmeal cookies that they would put fruit, nuts and things in, all the raisins in it. And oh, and I forgot, in the morning when he made the bread, started his bread, that's when he made all his pies for noon. At the noon meal was when they had their pies. And he would also bake his cakes in the afternoon because of his evening for supper with fruit and cake was their dessert. Now in all of those tables were always set with five different kinds of pastries. It was at every setting on the table. Of course they had their two kinds of bread, light and dark bread. At noon there was the pies and in the evening it was the cakes. There was never less than two kinds of meat, always. Two different kinds of meat on the table. There was always two different kinds of vegetables. And then of course you had your potatoes. In the evening there was always soup for the one who wanted the soup. They really fed the lumberjacks good. They were the best fed men in the country. And they, I forgot what I was going to say. Oh, and then in the evening, after supper, of course supper was always at 5 o'clock. The men come on in and would wash and clean up. There's never a lumberjack left of the table dirty. He always changed his shirt. He had a clean shirt when he came in to eat. And of course they would eat at five o'clock. Well, at the door as they would go out from the cookhouse, it depend on what time of the season it was. If it was in, when the peaches was on, there'd be cases of peaches sitting on each side of the door, or cases of apples. There was always oranges there. They could always pick up their orange, because they went out too long before they went to bed. If there were strawberries, some of them were strawberry cases. It was always just fruit, plums, pears, apples. And I just about took care of it, and of course the oranges resolve us there, regardless of what fruit was there. Before the men went to bed, about 8 o'clock, at 9 o'clock everything was quiet. Everybody was in bed. By 8 o'clock, the boys could always come to the cookhouse, and the coffee was on. They could get their cup of coffee before they went to bed, could go and help themselves to any cookies, cake that was left over, or pie that was left over. Whatever they desired, they could have their lunch before they went to bed. And at 9 o'clock, it was lights out, everything. Everybody was in bed at 9 o'clock. But the cooks got up at four o'clock In order to have breakfast breakfast usually was at six o'clock Because it's seven They took out for the woods So you see they put in long hours. Well that was when they had the teams. Of course the teamsters had longer hours because then the drivers took care of the horses. They had to go down and feed their horses. They curried their horses every morning. They would bring them in at night and unharnessed them, watered them, or they would water them while they were still in the harness, take them to the barn and unharness them, and always curried their horses after. Currying is brushing them and cleaning them off real good, feeding them, giving them their oats and their hay before the driver ever went in to wash and clean up to go to supper. And those drivers really took care of their horses. They took good pride in their horses. Of course, they needed the horses to skid the logs and get the logs down to wherever, to the chute or wherever they were going to take them. Well, then as the years went by, of course, they, then they got the trucks. And they were little old single-wheel trucks with maybe double wheels on the back, all hard tires. No springs on the things. It was the worst when riding above board. But it took the logs. And of course, the longer logs then, they had just the chassis and the front wheels, they had to have trailers on them for those 10 and 12 foot logs, but there was no brakes for the trailer. So the boys, the blacksmith, ten days there was a blacksmith in every camp. He made the PVs. He made everything they used. The sharp things, they even the cables, they made the hooks on the cables, and they even braided the cables around those hooks. A good blacksmith knew how to open those cable wires and braid them to go around the hook on the end of the cable. And then of course the blacksmith made what they called shoes that fit right under, they were iron plates, that fit right under the back trailer tires. And then that was wired to the front of the back of the truck, so they wouldn't slip out from under the wheels. And that would drag down the hill. That was their break, when they took a load down the hill. Then at the bottom of the hill, the driver had to stop, back his truck off of that, load it, Or sometimes they just left him there if he didn't have another heel. He would leave him there and pick him up on his way back to go on up. And that was the way they loved in the early times. And they thought they were lucky when they were able to get the trucks, that they didn't have to do so much with horses. Well, then, of course, at our time, we were at that time, Mr. Johnson, Mr. Larson was up the St. Joe, what they called father house on the St. Joe. Of course we had what they called the rag camps, which was your tent camps. And of course, I think that then he hired – we didn't own any of the trucks. We hired – I think there was twelve trucks that come on in there. And of course, you had your loaders and you would load the trucks and took them on down and those boys put in long hours. The truck drivers, they would change the drivers and they'd have two shifts. They started the boys at four o'clock in the morning and when that shift come in at night, the other boys took over And they went all night long. I remember one time we had an elderly man working in our camp, and he was a truck driver, and three of his sons was working for us. Two of them was top loaders and one was a hooker. But the old man had the truck and this one night, always the last truck that went down in the evening, next to the last one, would always wait at the landing for the last truck so if nothing had happened, they'd be together, that he wouldn't be left there alone. And he got on in this night at, I think it was one o'clock, he got back into camp. And he went to, of course, he didn't even take time to wash. He was so tired he went right to bed. Well, when Mr. Larson was waking up the other shift at 2 o'clock, He made a mistake and woke up old Miller. And Miller still had his hat on in bed, and he'd come to the wash house, and he still had his hat on. And one of the boys was kidding him about it. And he says, well, by God, a man don't have to stay long in this camp to stay overnight. You know, he'd been there one hour in bed. Now that's the hours those loggers used to put in. It was nothing for 18 and 16 hours, day after day, and those men thought nothing of it. We girls, I worked that one summer in the camp. I had 54 settings at my table, but 53 men sat down. We always kept one empty setting for someone to come in. Every man had his own place at the table, and nobody else ever sat in his place either. And the drivers now, of course, would start out, everybody would start out at 7 o'clock. But at noon, we would take the lunches up, we had to climb up the hill to where the road was. We stood there with the men's lunches. We made the lunches and gave them to the drivers as the trucks went by. Well then of course in the evening they wouldn't be in for supper. The ones that were going down, we'd give them lunches, but the ones that come back then would be done for the day and they could eat in the cookhouse. But the trucks that were going down, we'd give them lunches again, eat on the way down. And then of course when they come back, they could go to cookhouse. The cookhouse after the supper meal, whatever was left over went on the hot plate on the stove and so on the steam plate. And the coffee of course was always on in ten gallon containers. The coffee was always on ten-gallon containers. And of course, we heroes that set the table up, the coffee pots, all set on a ring on hooks around over the top of the coffee pot and they had no lids on the coffee pots. They took all the lids off. And we would have to take those coffee pots and dip the coffee out of these big tin-gallon vats and pour the coffee in the coffee pots and take them to the table. But those were always full of coffee, there was always coffee, excepting after 8 o'clock that coffee was poured into coffee pots and was put on the stove for the ones that would come in that one hour, excepting the truck drivers, then it was all night on that. That is when they had the all-nighters going. But they put in long hours and yet they loved their work and it was dangerous work. Them days a truck driver was never allowed on the truck without a PB, an axe, a shovel, a bucket, which was a collapsible bucket, and his water bag. Because if there was a fire in the woods, you had to have your bucket to get it to crisp. But that was a must on every truck. You see, no driver ever got on a truck that he didn't have cocked shoes. Them days, every one of the loggers had cock shoes. Now you see men in the woods in Oxford. It's been years since I've seen cock shoes. They just don't wear them anymore. And then days, it was a must, you just didn't go in the woods without caught shoes. But that year up St. Joe, Mr. Johnson had a payroll of 183 men And they put in 20 million feet of logs. They hauled them to St. Joe and they went in the river and then was boomed down the river to St. Mary and most of them come to Coeur d'Alene because that was a, if I remember right, that was a Whitten job. So the gloves all come to Coeur d'Alene to Whitten's. But they just run them loose down the St. Joe but boomed them after they got to St. Mary's. And then they come down in the booms from St. Mary's down the river to, I believe that is at Rose Lake, isn't it? And then they boomed them there. I think they had a sorting gap there at Rose Lake someplace that they boomed them. Or was that down the Coeur d'Alene River? They boomed them at Rose Lake because they used to have the sorting gaps in the rivers and then they would have men on their logs that would divide the logs. Every log had a stamp on it. You knew which mill it went to. And whichever mill it went to went into its own boom. And they always had the men on the river who would be walking the logs and would sort out the logs. But I think I had that mixed up at Rose Lake, it was the ones that come down the Coeur d'Alene River where they boomed them. The ones that come down from the St. Joe was at Harrison, they boomed them. At Harrison, that's right, that's where they boomed him. And then from there, Mr. Johnson came back to Coeur d'Alene and logged out here at the log cabins in the Fourth of July Canyon down to Armstrong's. He loved that whole canyon out there.

lines.line: # Introduction and Early Life

> The interview begins with Sadie Brooeon introducing Elsie Lenz Johnson. The discussion outlines Elsie's early life experiences and her first meeting with her husband, Mr. Johnson, both living on farms in Cougar Gulch. They relied on logging and making cordwood for their livelihood.
** introduction, early life, Cougar Gulch, farm life, Elsie Lenz Johnson **
keywords: ** introduction, early life, Cougar Gulch, farm life, Elsie Lenz Johnson **

metadata.title: Introduction and Early Life
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with Sadie Brooeon introducing Elsie Lenz Johnson. The discussion outlines Elsie's early life experiences and her first meeting with her husband, Mr. Johnson, both living on farms in Cougar Gulch. They relied on logging and making cordwood for their livelihood.
metadata.keywords: introduction, early life, Cougar Gulch, farm life, Elsie Lenz Johnson

lines.line: # Logging and Sledding

> Elsie describes the seasonal activities of logging and making cordwood. The Johnson family used sleds and horses to transport logs during winter as sleds could carry more than wagons. Despite the challenges and lack of brakes on the sleds, there were no accidents.
** logging, sledding, winter, horses, transportation **

metadata.title: Logging and Sledding
metadata.synopsis: Elsie describes the seasonal activities of logging and making cordwood. The Johnson family used sleds and horses to transport logs during winter as sleds could carry more than wagons. Despite the challenges and lack of brakes on the sleds, there were no accidents.

lines.line: # Expanding Logging Operations

> The Johnson family transitions to larger lumber operations with Wittens' mill. The shift includes using more advanced methods such as trains for transporting logs to mills, as well as securing contracts and renting horses for big jobs at logging camps.
** expansion, Wittens mill, train transportation, logging contracts, horses **
keywords: ** expansion, Wittens mill, train transportation, logging contracts, horses **

metadata.title: Expanding Logging Operations
metadata.synopsis: The Johnson family transitions to larger lumber operations with Wittens' mill. The shift includes using more advanced methods such as trains for transporting logs to mills, as well as securing contracts and renting horses for big jobs at logging camps.
metadata.keywords: expansion, Wittens mill, train transportation, logging contracts, horses

lines.line: # Advanced Logging Techniques
> Elsie details the use of flumes for transporting logs and recounts an accident involving Mr. Johnson. Recovery from injuries required community effort and highlighted the dangers of logging work.
** flumes, logging techniques, accident, recovery, community effort **
keywords: ** flumes, logging techniques, accident, recovery, community effort **

lines.line: # Camp Life and Cooking

> The chapter expands on life in tent camps, detailing the organization of these camps with wooden floors and tents, the role of camp cooks, and the logistics of providing meals for a large number of workers.
** camp life, tent camps, cooking, organization, meals **

** meal preparations, baked goods, meats, camp routines, hygiene **
